statement.markdown: You are given a string representing a directory path and an array of directory names. You need to write a function that returns all directory paths in the given directory and its subdirectories. The function should use recursion to traverse the directory tree.
statement.notes: Note that the directory path is absolute and the array of directories is ordered, so the first directory in the array should be the top-most directory.

constraints.description: The number of directories in the array

examples.explanation: The directory /home/user has two subdirectories: documents and pictures.
The root directory has many subdirectories.

hints.text: Use recursion to traverse the directory tree.

editorial.title: Editorial: Recursive Directory Walker
editorial.intuition: The function should use recursion to traverse the directory tree. The base case is when the array of directories is empty, in which case the function should return an array containing the current directory path. Otherwise, the function should recursively call itself with the current directory path and the rest of the array of directories.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force solution would involve using a loop to iterate over the array of directories and manually construct the directory paths.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would involve using recursion to traverse the directory tree.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution would involve using recursion to traverse the directory tree and using an array to store the directory paths.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The function is correct because it uses recursion to traverse the directory tree and returns an array containing all directory paths in the given directory and its subdirectories.
editorial.dryRun: Example run: If the input is '/home/user' and the array of directories is ['documents', 'pictures'], the function should return [ '/home/user', '/home/user/documents', '/home/user/pictures'].
commonMistakes: Not handling the base case correctly
